name: archetypes/comparison
title: Comparison
description: Exactly two columns with aligned rows — options, before/after, us/them — and an optional one-line verdict. Alignment by construction keeps the fight fair.
useCases: presenting a decision between two options with honest trade-offs
before/after proof that a change worked

columns.description: Exactly two ls-layout__region columns; a third option is a table, not a comparison.

verdict.description: Optional one-line verdict after the columns; if the deck makes a call, say it.

motion.notes: Columns enter with the core stagger. When you add a verdict line, give it ls-reveal with data-step="1" so the call lands after both sides are seen.

icons.guidance: At most one sprite icon per column heading, and only if both columns get one.

useWhen: a decision between exactly two options with real trade-offs
before/after evidence where the same measure appears on both sides
avoidWhen: three or more options — use components/table so rows stay comparable
one side is a strawman; an honest comparison gives both columns their best case

alternatives.when: the contrast is one number moving
more than two options or many criteria

composition.copyGuidance: 2-4 rows per column, aligned across both; unequal rows read as a rigged fight. Rows may be list items, stats, or layout text - keep the same construct on both sides.

attributes.name: data-ls-archetype
attributes.description: Marks the slide for contract linting and deck scorecards.

usage: Keep rows parallel: row N answers the same question in both columns.
Use ls-layout__note for a per-column bottom line ("Lower risk · slower"); the subgrid keeps the notes level.
For before/after, repeat the same stat label on both sides and let the values differ.
